title: The Extended Phenotype: The Long Reach of the Gene
excerpt: Dawkins's landmark sequel to The Selfish Gene, arguing that a gene's phenotypic effects extend beyond the individual body to shape the environment, artifacts, and even other organisms — a profound reframing of the gene-centered view of evolution.
keyIdeas: Phenotype is not bounded by the organism's body — gene effects reach into artifacts, landscapes, and other creatures
The active germ-line replicator is the true unit of selection, not the organism or group
Parasite manipulation of host behavior is a classic extended phenotype — the parasite's genes shaping the host's body and actions
Organisms are vehicles whose primary purpose is to propagate the replicators they carry
keyTakeaways: Genes synthesize only proteins directly; all other phenotypic effects are indirect extensions into the environment
Arms races between species can be understood as reciprocal extended phenotype manipulation
Selfish DNA and jumping genes are extended phenotypes of parasitic genetic elements within the genome
The organism itself is a puzzle requiring explanation: why do replicators band together in discrete bodies?
whoShouldRead: Biologists and advanced students of evolutionary theory
Readers of The Selfish Gene who want the deeper, more rigorous argument
Philosophers of biology interested in reductionism and levels of selection
Anyone curious about parasitism, niche construction, and ecosystem engineering
whoShouldSkip: Casual readers looking for light popular science — this is Dawkins's most technical book
Readers expecting another popular tour like The Selfish Gene — The Extended Phenotype is aimed at professionals
Those who reject gene-centered adaptationism outright
